raw_response: {"rating": 4.5, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is an opinion/editorial that clearly advances a right-leaning critique of higher education, mocking progressive academic trends and championing Western canon and practical curricula. The author uses derisive language toward “progressive orthodoxy” and “intersectionality” while lamenting moral decline and crime leniency, with no balancing perspectives. The consistent one-sided framing supports a Right rating at 4.5. Detailed Analysis: (1) Loaded, mocking descriptors frame academia negatively: the author calls Ivy League offerings “ridiculous,” labels courses “nonsensical progressive orthodoxy,” “intersectionality-laden,” and describes universities as “high on their own supply”; (2) Moral-decline and law-and-order framing appears in the author’s voice, tying a New York class about theft to perceived shoplifting leniency and asking rhetorically about the city’s “sense of right and wrong”; (3) Advocacy for traditional canon and conservative cultural values: the author says colleges have lost their mission to form responsible citizens and insists “Young people need a grasp of the texts and ideas that make Western civilization great,” contrasting classics and calculus with “playing video games” and dissecting pop lyrics; (4) Selective curation without balance: the piece spotlights unusual or trendy courses at select schools to generalize about academia, presents cost figures and negative polls, but includes no defenses from educators or examples of rigorous traditional coursework currently offered; (5) Populist cost outrage framing: repeated emphasis on “eye-watering” tuition and imagined six-figure debts for questionable courses is used to indict higher education broadly; (6) Sarcasm and rhetorical questions (“I guess it’s fitting in New York…,” “Whatever happened to The Odyssey?”) further signal a culturally conservative, anti-progressive stance without presenting counterarguments."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an opinion/editorial that clearly advances a right-leaning critique of higher education, mocking progressive academic trends and championing Western canon and practical curricula. The author uses derisive language toward “progressive orthodoxy” and “intersectionality” while lamenting moral decline and crime leniency, with no balancing perspectives. The consistent one-sided framing supports a Right rating at 4.5. Detailed Analysis: (1) Loaded, mocking descriptors frame academia negatively: the author calls Ivy League offerings “ridiculous,” labels courses “nonsensical progressive orthodoxy,” “intersectionality-laden,” and describes universities as “high on their own supply”; (2) Moral-decline and law-and-order framing appears in the author’s voice, tying a New York class about theft to perceived shoplifting leniency and asking rhetorically about the city’s “sense of right and wrong”; (3) Advocacy for traditional canon and conservative cultural values: the author says colleges have lost their mission to form responsible citizens and insists “Young people need a grasp of the texts and ideas that make Western civilization great,” contrasting classics and calculus with “playing video games” and dissecting pop lyrics; (4) Selective curation without balance: the piece spotlights unusual or trendy courses at select schools to generalize about academia, presents cost figures and negative polls, but includes no defenses from educators or examples of rigorous traditional coursework currently offered; (5) Populist cost outrage framing: repeated emphasis on “eye-watering” tuition and imagined six-figure debts for questionable courses is used to indict higher education broadly; (6) Sarcasm and rhetorical questions (“I guess it’s fitting in New York…,” “Whatever happened to The Odyssey?”) further signal a culturally conservative, anti-progressive stance without presenting counterarguments.

explanation: This is an opinion/editorial that clearly advances a right-leaning critique of higher education, mocking progressive academic trends and championing Western canon and practical curricula. The author uses derisive language toward “progressive orthodoxy” and “intersectionality” while lamenting moral decline and crime leniency, with no balancing perspectives. The consistent one-sided framing supports a Right rating at 4.5. Detailed Analysis: (1) Loaded, mocking descriptors frame academia negatively: the author calls Ivy League offerings “ridiculous,” labels courses “nonsensical progressive orthodoxy,” “intersectionality-laden,” and describes universities as “high on their own supply”; (2) Moral-decline and law-and-order framing appears in the author’s voice, tying a New York class about theft to perceived shoplifting leniency and asking rhetorically about the city’s “sense of right and wrong”; (3) Advocacy for traditional canon and conservative cultural values: the author says colleges have lost their mission to form responsible citizens and insists “Young people need a grasp of the texts and ideas that make Western civilization great,” contrasting classics and calculus with “playing video games” and dissecting pop lyrics; (4) Selective curation without balance: the piece spotlights unusual or trendy courses at select schools to generalize about academia, presents cost figures and negative polls, but includes no defenses from educators or examples of rigorous traditional coursework currently offered; (5) Populist cost outrage framing: repeated emphasis on “eye-watering” tuition and imagined six-figure debts for questionable courses is used to indict higher education broadly; (6) Sarcasm and rhetorical questions (“I guess it’s fitting in New York…,” “Whatever happened to The Odyssey?”) further signal a culturally conservative, anti-progressive stance without presenting counterarguments.
